title: 1.1: Concludes that metrics can be trusted without categorizing any as fundamentally unreliable (e.g., does not label behaviors as bugs, issues to fix, or broken)
1.2 Affirms that timing values can be trusted for performance analysis (e.g., does not categorize metrics as "unreliable", "low trust", or "cannot be trusted")
1.3 Explains that zero connecting/TLS time on subsequent requests indicates connection reuse (e.g., connection pooling, no handshake needed for reused connections)
1.4 Explains that variable "blocked" timing reflects connection pool wait time (e.g., waiting for available connection, pool contention)
1.5 States that the Windows issue is not a race condition that needs fixing (e.g., OS timer limitation causes identical timestamps)
1.6 Explains that multiple ConnectStart/ConnectDone calls are due to dual stack/Happy Eyeballs dialing (e.g., IPv4/IPv6 simultaneous connection attempts)
1.7 States that the multiple hook calls do not cause measurement errors (e.g., only first timestamp is recorded, no double counting)
1.8 Explains that the Reused=false with matching timestamps scenario is a documented Go stdlib HTTP/2 quirk (e.g., abandoned connections get pooled and reused)
